# Functional Design Document: Humanoid Robotics Physical AI Data & Compute Infrastructure Platform

**Document type:** Functional Design Document (FDD)
**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** 2026-06-11
**Source:** Functional Requirements Document (FRD) v1.0, 2026-06-10 (`frd.md`)
**Language:** English

---

## 0. Domain Concept & Requirements Knowledge Base

This chapter records the outcome of Phase 1 (iterative deep reading of the FRD) and Phase 2 (module-by-module external research). It is the normative vocabulary and conceptual model for every subsequent chapter. All module designs in Chapters 1–3 reference the concepts defined here; no module redefines them.

### 0.1 Reading Methodology

Three full passes were performed over the FRD:

1. **Pass 1 — Concept extraction.** Every business concept, domain entity, actor, and process flow was extracted verbatim and normalized (Sections 0.2–0.4).
2. **Pass 2 — Relationship mapping.** Entities, features, and subsystems were connected into a Functional Requirements Knowledge Graph: feature→entity dependencies, module→module data/event exchange, and authorization boundaries (Section 0.5).
3. **Pass 3 — Gap and ambiguity analysis.** Ambiguities, implicit assumptions, and under-specified areas (including FRD Section 15 "Open Decisions") were resolved into explicit, documented design assumptions (Section 0.6). Per the scope-discipline constraint, each resolution is an interpretation of FRD intent, not new scope.

### 0.2 Business Concept Inventory

#### 0.2.1 Platform mission concept

The platform operationalizes a single macro-process, the **Physical AI closed loop**:

> Capture physical robot data → curate and govern data assets → generate or vary training/evaluation scenarios → train and evaluate models → package release candidates → analyze failures → feed new data requirements back into collection and simulation.

Everything in the FRD is an elaboration of one of seven loop stations: (1) Capture, (2) Curate/Govern, (3) Expand (simulation/synthetic), (4) Train, (5) Evaluate, (6) Release, (7) Analyze Failures. The loop is closed by converting station-7 outputs into station-1/2/3/4 work items.

#### 0.2.2 Subsystem concepts (FRD §6)

| Code | Subsystem | Owns (data ownership boundary) |
|---|---|---|
| A | Physical Data Factory & Asset Governance | Raw recordings, episodes, annotations, dataset snapshots, data quality status, data lineage |
| B | Simulation & Synthetic Data Factory | Scenarios, scenario collections, simulation runs, synthetic data batches, synthetic validation status |
| C | Physical AI ModelOps & Evaluation | Training plans/runs, model candidates, evaluation suites/reports, release candidates, failure records |
| D | Workflow Orchestration & Compute Operations | Workflow templates/runs, work requests, queues, quotas, utilization, operational history |
| X | Integrated Physical AI Workbench | No business assets of its own; a cross-cutting synchronized 4D review surface over A–D assets |

#### 0.2.3 Platform-management concepts (mandated design extensions)

The FRD specifies tenancy, RBAC, and audit at requirement level (FR-D5.x) without prescribing mechanisms. The design mandate for this FDD fixes four mechanisms, which this knowledge base treats as **first-class concepts**:

1. **Email-code login.** Users authenticate with a one-time random code sent to a corporate email address that a Platform Administrator pre-registered. No self-registration; no passwords.
2. **Tenant.** The unit of hard isolation for both **infrastructure resources** (compute, storage) and **business resources** (data, code references, projects, tasks/workflows, models). Tenants bind resources in two forms: **physical** (a specific GPU machine; a specific storage volume/filesystem) and **logical** (a compute service such as a Ray cluster instance; a storage service such as an object-storage bucket/endpoint).
3. **Project.** A finer-grained business-resource container under a tenant. Business assets (campaigns, datasets, scenarios, models, workflows) belong to exactly one project; projects belong to exactly one tenant.
4. **Built-in roles + atomic permissions.** Four built-in roles — Platform Administrator, Tenant Administrator, Operations Engineer, R&D Engineer — composed from modular atomic permission units that can be re-combined into custom roles.

#### 0.2.4 Concept reconciliation: FRD personas vs. built-in roles

The FRD lists ten business personas (§7); the mandate fixes four built-in authorization roles. These are reconciled as **two layers**:

- **Authorization layer (built-in roles):** determines what a user *may* do (permission evaluation). See M03.
- **Persona layer (business personas):** determines what a user *does* (UI defaults, work routing, dashboards). Personas are implemented as **permission-set templates** applied on top of a built-in role at tenant/project scope.

| FRD persona | Default built-in role | Persona permission-set template (project-scoped unless noted) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Collection Lead | R&D Engineer | campaign:create/update, ingest:initiate, coverage:read |
| Robotics Data Engineer | R&D Engineer | ingest:*, quality:*, alignment:*, snapshot:publish, lineage:read |
| Annotator | R&D Engineer | annotation.task:read/submit, preannotation:review |
| Annotation QA Lead | R&D Engineer | annotation.project:*, qa:*, taxonomy:propose |
| Simulation Engineer | R&D Engineer | scenario:*, simrun:*, synthetic:*, validation:submit |
| Model Engineer | R&D Engineer | trainplan:*, trainrun:*, model:register, release:assemble |
| Evaluation Lead | R&D Engineer | evalsuite:*, evalrun:*, evalreport:approve, failure:triage |
| Platform Operator | Operations Engineer | (platform-scoped) workflow.ops:*, quota:*, queue:*, incident:* |
| Governance / Release Approver | Tenant Administrator (or R&D Engineer + grants) | approval:decide, audit:read, access-review:read |
| Product / Program Manager | R&D Engineer | report:read, coverage:read, milestone:read (read-mostly template) |

This mapping is a **documented design assumption** (see DA-01, §0.6).

### 0.3 Domain Entity Inventory

Thirty-one governed entity types. The first 18 come directly from FRD §11; the remainder are required by the mandated platform-management and analytics modules. Authoritative attribute-level schemas appear in each owning module's Data Design; this table fixes name, owner, and identity.

| # | Entity | Owning module | Identity / version rule | FRD anchor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Collection Campaign | M04 | `cmp-{ulid}`; mutable until archived; status machine §M04 | §11, A1 |
| 2 | Raw Recording | M05 | `rec-{ulid}`; **immutable after acceptance** (BR-01) | §11, A2 |
| 3 | Episode / Trajectory | M06 | `epi-{ulid}`; derived; re-derivation creates new version | §11, A4 |
| 4 | Annotation Project | M07 | `anp-{ulid}`; mutable config + status machine | §11, A6 |
| 5 | Annotation Set | M07 | `ans-{ulid}` + integer version; versions immutable | §11, A6 |
| 6 | Dataset Snapshot | M08 | `dss-{ulid}` + semver; **immutable once published** (BR-04) | §11, A8 |
| 7 | Scenario | M09 | `scn-{ulid}` + integer version | §11, B1 |
| 8 | Scenario Collection | M09 | `scc-{ulid}` + integer version | B1.5 |
| 9 | Simulation Run | M10 | `sim-{ulid}`; immutable record after terminal state | §11, B2 |
| 10 | Synthetic Data Batch | M10 | `syn-{ulid}`; immutable payload; mutable validation status | §11, B3/B4 |
| 11 | Training Plan | M11 | `tpl-{ulid}`; mutable until approved, then frozen | §11, C1 |
| 12 | Training Run | M11 | `trn-{ulid}`; immutable record after terminal state | §11, C2 |
| 13 | Model Candidate | M12 | `mdl-{ulid}` + monotonically increasing version per model family | §11, C3 |
| 14 | Evaluation Suite | M13 | `evs-{ulid}` + integer version | §11, C4 |
| 15 | Evaluation Report | M13 | `evr-{ulid}`; **immutable after approval** (BR-08); superseded-by links | §11, C4 |
| 16 | Release Candidate | M14 | `rls-{ulid}`; evidence-frozen at submission; status machine §M14 | §11, C5 |
| 17 | Failure Record | M15 | `fal-{ulid}`; mutable triage fields, immutable source evidence | §11, C6 |
| 18 | Work Request | M16 | `wrq-{ulid}`; status machine §M16 | §11, D2 |
| 19 | Workflow Template | M16 | `wft-{ulid}` + unique name per tenant + integer version; versions immutable (FR-D1.7) | D1 |
| 20 | Workflow Run | M16 | `wfr-{ulid}`; immutable record after terminal state | §11, D1 |
| 21 | Tenant | M02 | `tnt-{ulid}` | D5.1 + mandate |
| 22 | Project | M02 | `prj-{ulid}`; belongs to exactly one tenant | D5.1/D5.5 + mandate |
| 23 | Resource Binding (compute/storage × physical/logical) | M02 | `rbd-{ulid}` | mandate |
| 24 | Quota Policy | M17 | `qta-{ulid}`; versioned | D3 |
| 25 | User Account | M03 | `usr-{ulid}`; email unique | D5 + mandate |
| 26 | Role / Permission Unit / Role Binding | M03 | `rol-{ulid}` / `prm-{key}` / `rbn-{ulid}` | D5.2 + mandate |
| 27 | Login Code & Session | M01 | code: hashed, single-use; session: `ses-{ulid}` | mandate |
| 28 | Audit Event | M03 (write API: all modules) | `aud-{ulid}`; append-only | D5.4, BR-14 |
| 29 | Data Cohort | M18 | `coh-{ulid}` + integer version | A7.4, X1.7 |
| 30 | Embedding & Vector Index entry | M18 | `emb-{asset_id}:{model}:{version}` | mandate (analytics) |
| 31 | Distribution Job / Distribution Report, Saved View | M18/M19 | `dst-{ulid}` / `viw-{ulid}` | mandate (analytics), X1.7 |

Supporting value objects (not independently governed, embedded in owners): Quality Rule, Quality Report, Alignment Tolerance Policy, Ingest Manifest, Remediation Task, Scenario Tag, Taxonomy Version, Dataset Card, Model Card, Approval Record, Incident Annotation, Service Class, Coverage Summary, Validation Report (synthetic), Variation Plan, Notification.

### 0.4 Actor & Process Inventory

#### 0.4.1 Actors

Human actors: the ten FRD personas (§0.2.4) plus the four built-in roles. System actors (non-human, run under service identities): Ingestion Worker, Intake Validator, Alignment Engine, Pre-annotation Generator, Embedding Indexer, Distribution Computer, Simulation Executor, Synthetic Generator, Training Executor, Evaluation Executor, Packaging Worker, Workflow Scheduler, Quota Enforcer, Notification Dispatcher, Audit Shipper, OTP Mailer. Each system actor is specified in Chapter 3 (Backend Service Designs).

#### 0.4.2 Business processes (FRD §8) and their module routes

| Process | Route through modules |
|---|---|
| BP-01 Real data → governed snapshot | M04 → M05 → M05(quality) → M06(align/segment) → M07(annotate/QA) → M08(snapshot) → consumable by M11/M13/M18 |
| BP-02 Coverage gap → simulation/synthetic dataset | M15/M18(gap) → M09(scenario) → M10(run+generate) → M10(validate) → M08(synthetic snapshot) |
| BP-03 Snapshot → model candidate | M11(plan, restriction check via M08) → M16(workflow) → M11(run tracking) → M12(register) |
| BP-04 Candidate evaluation → release decision | M13(suite/plan) → M16(execute) → M13(report) → M15(failure clusters) → M14(release candidate) → approval |
| BP-05 Closed-loop failure management | M15(intake from M07/M10/M13/M14/external logs) → triage → work items in M16 → verification links back to M13 regression sets |
| BP-06 Workflow & compute operations | M16(request/approve/schedule) → M17(quota/priority) → M16(execute) → output registration into owning modules → M17(attribution) → M03(audit) |

#### 0.4.3 Login & administration processes (mandated)

- **BP-M1 User onboarding:** Platform Administrator pre-registers corporate email + assigns role/tenant/project bindings → user requests login code → OTP mailed → user verifies → session issued.
- **BP-M2 Tenant provisioning:** Platform Administrator creates tenant → binds physical/logical compute and storage resources → appoints Tenant Administrator → Tenant Administrator creates projects and assigns members.

### 0.5 Functional Requirements Knowledge Graph

#### 0.5.1 Feature → entity dependency matrix (condensed)

Each FRD requirement family is listed with the entities it reads (R) and writes (W).

| Requirement family | Entities written | Entities read |
|---|---|---|
| A1 Campaigns | Collection Campaign | Tenant, Project, User |
| A2 Ingestion | Raw Recording, Ingest Manifest | Campaign, Resource Binding (storage) |
| A3 Intake validation | Quality Report, Remediation Task, quality status on Raw Recording | Raw Recording, Quality Rule |
| A4 Alignment | Episode (+ lineage edges) | Raw Recording, Alignment Tolerance Policy |
| A5 Segmentation/tagging | Episode segments, Scenario Tags, Taxonomy Version | Episode, Raw Recording |
| A6 Annotation & QA | Annotation Project, Annotation Set, pre-annotation records | Episode, Cohort, Taxonomy Version |
| A7 Catalog & mining | Cohort | every asset entity (read), Audit Event (W on access) |
| A8 Snapshots & governance | Dataset Snapshot, Dataset Card, deprecation links | Episode, Annotation Set, Synthetic Data Batch, usage policies |
| B1 Scenario catalog | Scenario, Scenario Collection | Failure Record, Episode, Coverage Summary |
| B2 Simulation runs | Simulation Run | Scenario, Model Candidate (optional) |
| B3/B4 Synthetic generation & validation | Synthetic Data Batch, Validation Report | Scenario, Dataset Snapshot (source), generation settings |
| B5 Real↔sim loop | links: Failure→Scenario, Sim→CollectionTask | Failure Record, Simulation Run |
| C1/C2 Training | Training Plan, Training Run | Dataset Snapshot, Evaluation Suite, Quota Policy, approvals |
| C3 Registry | Model Candidate, Model Card | Training Run, Evaluation Report |
| C4 Evaluation | Evaluation Suite, Evaluation Report | Model Candidate, Dataset Snapshot, Scenario Collection, real-world trial evidence records |
| C5 Release | Release Candidate, Approval Record | Model Candidate, Evaluation Report, Dataset lineage |
| C6 Failure loop | Failure Record, regression set (as Evaluation Suite subtype + Cohort) | Evaluation Report, Simulation Run, QA results, post-release logs |
| D1/D2 Orchestration & requests | Workflow Template/Run, Work Request, Approval Record | all asset entities as parameters; Quota Policy |
| D3 Compute allocation | Quota Policy, utilization records | Tenant, Project, Resource Binding, Workflow Run |
| D4 Monitoring | Incident Annotation, alerts | Workflow Run, Quota Policy, Evaluation Report |
| D5 Tenancy/roles/audit | Tenant, Project, Role Binding, Audit Event | User, Role, Permission Unit |
| X Workbench | comments, review decisions, Saved Views, failure tags | Episode, Annotation Set, Simulation Run, Evaluation Report, Model Candidate |
| Mandate: Login | Login Code, Session | User Account |
| Mandate: Analytics & Mining | Embedding entries, Distribution Report, Cohort | Episode, Dataset Snapshot, Scenario Tags, Annotation Set, Failure Record |

#### 0.5.2 Module → module event/data exchange (edge list)

Directed edges `producer → consumer : payload` (full interface bindings in Chapter 1.4):

- M01 → all modules : authenticated session context (user_id, tenant_id, role bindings)
- M02 → M16/M17 : tenant/project resource bindings (where workflows may run, where data may be stored)
- M03 → all modules : permission decisions (`authz.check`), audit ingestion (`audit.append`)
- M04 → M05 : campaign context for ingestion sessions
- M05 → M06 : accepted recordings (quality status `accepted` / `accepted_with_warnings`)
- M05 → M15 : quality failures convertible to remediation/failure records
- M06 → M07 : episodes available for annotation tasks
- M06 → M18 : episodes available for embedding/indexing and distribution analysis
- M07 → M08 : approved annotation sets eligible for snapshot membership
- M08 → M11/M13 : published snapshots selectable for training/evaluation (restriction-gated)
- M08 → M18 : snapshot composition for coverage/distribution reporting
- M09 → M10 : approved scenarios/collections for runs and generation
- M10 → M08 : validated synthetic batches publishable as synthetic/mixed snapshots
- M10 → M13 : simulation evaluation evidence
- M11 → M12 : completed training runs for candidate registration
- M12 → M13 : candidates submitted for evaluation
- M13 → M14 : approved evaluation reports as release evidence
- M13/M10/M07/M14 → M15 : failure events (evaluation, simulation, QA, post-release)
- M15 → M04/M05/M07/M09/M11/M13/M18 : remediation work items (collection/remediation tasks, annotation corrections, scenario requests, retraining plans, suite/regression updates, mining cohorts) via M16 work requests
- M16 → M17 : scheduling requests, resource consumption records
- M16 → M04…M14 : registered workflow outputs (assets) on completion (FR-D1.4)
- M17 → M16 : admission decisions (quota pass/deny/escalate)
- M18 → M09/M13 : coverage gaps and distribution findings driving scenario and suite expansion
- M19 (Workbench) → M07/M13/M15 : review decisions, failure tags, comments, saved views/cohorts
- all modules → M20 (Notification) : alert triggers (workflow failure, quota breach, quality block, evaluation regression, approval blocker — FR-D4.4)

#### 0.5.3 Authorization boundary graph

Four nested scopes, evaluated in order: **Platform ⊃ Tenant ⊃ Project ⊃ Asset**.

- **Platform scope:** Platform Administrator (all permissions, all scopes — mandate); Operations Engineer (infrastructure-resource management permissions platform-wide: quotas, queues, bindings health, utilization; **no business-resource permissions** — mandate).
- **Tenant scope:** Tenant Administrator (all permissions within assigned tenant(s), including business resources and tenant-local resource binding management).
- **Project scope:** R&D Engineer (usage permissions on tenant compute/storage; full permissions on business resources within assigned projects).
- **Asset scope:** per-asset restrictions (privacy/license/usage tags on snapshots, restricted artifacts) override role grants — deny-wins (FR-A8.5, FR-D5.3, BR-05).

Boundary-blur resolution (mandate requirement): **M01 authenticates** (who you are) → **M02 scopes** (which tenant/project containers exist and which resources they may touch) → **M03 authorizes** (which atomic permissions your role bindings grant inside those containers, then asset-level restriction checks). Login never grants scope; tenancy never grants permission; permissions never cross tenant isolation.

### 0.6 Ambiguities, Gaps, and Documented Design Assumptions

Each assumption below resolves an FRD ambiguity or open decision. Tagged DA-xx and cross-referenced throughout the FDD. Per FRD §15, none of these block the design; per the scope-discipline rule, none add features beyond FRD + mandate.

| ID | Ambiguity / gap | Resolution adopted in this FDD |
|---|---|---|
| DA-01 | FRD has 10 personas; mandate has 4 built-in roles | Two-layer model: built-in roles for authorization, personas as permission-set templates (§0.2.4). Custom roles composable from atomic permission units. |
| DA-02 | FR-D5.1 says "workspace, project, or tenant" without fixing a hierarchy | Fixed hierarchy: Tenant → Project. "Workspace" is treated as a synonym for Project and not used as a separate concept anywhere in this FDD. |
| DA-03 | FRD silent on authentication mechanism | Mandate applied: pre-registered corporate email + one-time random code (OTP). No passwords, no self-registration, no third-party IdP in scope. SSO/OIDC is explicitly out of scope for v1. |
| DA-04 | OD-01 canonical episode schema | Internal canonical episode follows an RLDS-style structure (steps with observation/action/outcome fields + episode metadata), stored in columnar/tensor-friendly form; ROS 2 bag and LeRobot/RLDS-style datasets are importable; export targets are RLDS-style and archive formats. Schema detail in M06 Data Design. |
| DA-05 | OD-02 alignment tolerances | Alignment Tolerance Policy is a governed value object (per tenant, selectable per campaign/embodiment/modality), with per-modality-pair tolerance entries; episodes failing policy are flagged `alignment_failed` and excluded from snapshot eligibility (FR-A4.3). |
| DA-06 | OD-03 taxonomy governance | Scenario/action/failure taxonomies are tenant-scoped versioned objects; QA Leads propose, Tenant Administrators approve; historical tags keep their taxonomy version (FR-A5.6). |
| DA-07 | OD-04 synthetic approval criteria | Validation thresholds are configurable per tenant via Validation Rubrics; platform enforces *presence and outcome* of validation, not specific scientific thresholds (FR-B4.1/B4.2). |
| DA-08 | OD-05 release approval policy | Approval matrix configurable per tenant; default: any change to artifact, dataset lineage, deployment class, or limitations re-triggers review (FR-C5.6). |
| DA-09 | OD-06 real-world trial evidence | Ingested as **Trial Evidence Records** through a governed upload + structured metadata form in M13; platform never executes robot trials (FR-C4.2, FR-C5.4). |
| DA-10 | OD-07 quota model | Quotas attach to Tenant (hard ceiling) and Project (sub-allocation); attribution dimensions: project, team, campaign, model, workflow, cost center (FR-D3.6). |
| DA-11 | OD-08 retention | Retention policies are per-asset-class settings (tenant-configurable) with statuses `active / archived / purged-metadata-retained`; raw recordings default to indefinite retention (BR-01). |
| DA-12 | "Streamed capture" mode (FR-A2.2) undefined | Implemented as **collection sessions**: chunked, resumable uploads bound to an open campaign; not a real-time robot control channel (out of scope §4.2). |
| DA-13 | Pre-annotation generators (FR-A6.2) unspecified | Generators are registered workflow job types (VLM captioner, detector, segmenter, action-segmenter); platform stores generator identity/version/confidence per FR-A6.3 but does not bundle specific models. |
| DA-14 | Notification channels (FR-D4.4) | In-app notification center + email; per-user subscription preferences. |
| DA-15 | Mandated Analytics & Mining vs. A7 catalog search overlap | Boundary fixed: M08 owns *governed catalog browse + scalar filtering*; M18 owns *hybrid scalar+vector retrieval, visualization, distribution computation, clustering*. M08 search delegates to M18's query engine for vector-augmented queries. |
| DA-16 | Mandate's "code" as tenant business resource | Interpreted as *code references* (repository URLs + commit hashes on training runs/workflow templates, FR-C3.3), not a hosted SCM. |
| DA-17 | Email infrastructure for OTP | Platform sends via configured corporate SMTP/ESP relay; deliverability monitoring is operational, out of FDD scope beyond send-status logging. |
| DA-18 | Evaluation execution for simulation (FR-C4.2) | Simulation evaluation executes through M10 runs with `purpose=evaluation`; offline dataset evaluation executes as M16 workflow with evaluation executor; both report into M13. |
| DA-19 | Post-release logs (FR-C5.7, FR-C6.1) arrive from outside | Ingested as governed **post-release log evidence** files attached to released Model Candidates via M14; platform performs no fleet telemetry collection (out of scope §4.2). |
| DA-20 | GPU machine / Ray cluster binding semantics | Physical binding = exclusive or shared attachment of an inventoried machine to one tenant; logical binding = endpoint + credential + capacity descriptor for an externally operated service (Ray cluster, object store). The platform *brokers and accounts*; it does not provision hardware. |

### 0.7 Research Findings & External References

Phase 2 research enriched each module family with current authoritative patterns. Findings are grouped by module family; each finding states the pattern adopted and the source. Sources were selected for authority (official docs, peer-recognized projects) and de-duplicated.


#### 0.7.1 Identity, Multi-Tenancy & Access Control (M01–M03)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NIST SP 800-63B §5.1.3.1 states that methods which do not prove possession of a specific device, "such as voice-over-IP (VOIP) or email, SHALL NOT be used for out-of-band authentication"; email OTP therefore does not count as a possession factor. | M01 treats email OTP as a single-factor (AAL1-class) convenience login suitable for an internal/B2B platform; the FDD must document this limitation and define a step-up path (enterprise SSO/OIDC or TOTP second factor) for privileged operations such as tenant resource administration. | [NIST SP 800-63B §5.1.3](https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-3/sp800-63b.html) |
| 2 | For out-of-band secrets, NIST 800-63B §5.1.3.2 requires the verifier to generate random secrets with ≥20 bits of entropy (≈6 decimal digits) using an approved RBG, accept a given secret only once (single use), and treat the authentication as invalid if not completed within 10 minutes; if the secret has <64 bits of entropy, a rate-limiting mechanism is mandatory, and §5.2.2 caps consecutive failed attempts per account at no more than 100. | M01 OTP spec: 6-digit (or 8-digit) CSPRNG code, single-use, server-side TTL of 5–10 min, per-code verify-attempt limit (e.g., 5) plus account-level throttling with exponential backoff well below the NIST 100-attempt ceiling; resending invalidates prior codes. | [NIST SP 800-63B §5.1.3.2 / §5.2.2](https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-3/sp800-63b.html) |
| 3 | AWS's reference OTP architecture stores only a salted hash of the one-time code with expiry metadata (never plaintext) and throttles both code-generation requests per destination and verification attempts per code. | M01 persists OTPs as salted hashes (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256 with server secret) keyed by email + purpose, with issue-rate limits per email and per source IP; a DB leak must not expose live codes. | [Build a Secure One-Time Password Architecture with AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/messaging-and-targeting/build-a-secure-one-time-password-architecture-with-aws/) |
| 4 | NIST SP 800-63B-4 session guidance: overall reauthentication timeout SHOULD be ≤30 days at AAL1 and ≤24 hours at AAL2 with an inactivity timeout ≤1 hour; after inactivity (but before overall timeout) reauthentication MAY use a single factor bound to the session secret. | M01 issues a short-lived access token (≤1 h) plus a rotating refresh token with an absolute lifetime (e.g., 7–30 days, configurable per deployment); idle expiry forces re-login via OTP; logout and credential change revoke refresh tokens server-side. | [NIST SP 800-63B-4 — AAL & Reauthentication](https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/sp800-63b/aal/) |
| 5 | Kubernetes' official multi-tenancy guidance uses namespace-per-tenant as the control-plane isolation unit, layered with RBAC, NetworkPolicy, and ResourceQuota; quotas cap a namespace's aggregate consumption (mitigating noisy neighbors) while LimitRange prevents a single object from monopolizing the namespace quota. | M02 maps each tenant to one (or more, per environment) dedicated namespace; tenant compute quotas in the FDD (CPU/memory/GPU counts) compile directly to ResourceQuota objects (including extended resources like nvidia.com/gpu) plus LimitRange defaults. | [Kubernetes Multi-tenancy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/multi-tenancy/) |
| 6 | Kubernetes taints repel pods from nodes unless pods carry a matching toleration; tolerations alone do not pin pods to those nodes, so dedicated-node patterns combine a node taint (enforcement) with node labels + nodeSelector/affinity (targeting) — the standard way to reserve GPU node pools for a specific group. | M02 "physical resource binding": assigning a GPU machine/node-pool to a tenant writes a tenant-scoped taint (e.g., tenant=acme:NoSchedule) and label on those nodes; the platform injects the matching toleration + nodeSelector only into that tenant's workloads, making exclusive physical binding scheduler-enforced rather than convention-based. | [Kubernetes Taints and Tolerations](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/scheduling-eviction/taint-and-toleration/) |
| 7 | Ray provides no strong multi-tenant isolation inside one cluster: Ray "resources" are logical only (not physically enforced), and any submitted job has full access to the whole cluster and its resources; the documented mitigation is one Ray cluster per tenant/workload, with the KubeRay operator running centrally and reconciling RayCluster CRs in each tenant's namespace. | M02 "logical resource binding": Ray/training/inference service instances are tenant-scoped cluster instances (RayCluster CRs) created inside the tenant's namespace and counted against its ResourceQuota — never shared Ray clusters across tenants; binding a logical service to a tenant = creating/attaching an instance in its namespace. | [Ray Clusters FAQ](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/cluster/faq.html); [Ray on Kubernetes](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/cluster/kubernetes/index.html) |
| 8 | Virtual clusters (vCluster) give each tenant its own API server and datastore running inside a host-cluster namespace — stronger control-plane isolation and tenant-defined CRDs — at the cost of extra control planes; namespace-based tenancy shares one control plane and is the resource-efficient choice for trusted/internal tenants. | The FDD adopts namespace-per-tenant as the baseline (tenants are vetted robotics customers, not arbitrary hostile parties) and records vCluster as the documented escalation path if a tenant later requires cluster-scoped APIs or custom operators. | [Kubernetes Namespaces vs. Virtual Clusters](https://www.vcluster.com/blog/kubernetes-namespaces-vs-virtual-clusters) |
| 9 | AWS documents three S3 multi-tenant patterns — bucket-per-tenant, shared bucket with prefix-per-tenant, and S3 Access Points; bucket-per-tenant gives the strongest isolation and per-tenant cost tags but hits the 100 (max 1,000) bucket account limit, while prefix-per-tenant scales to any tenant count using IAM policies conditioned on the tenant prefix; dynamically generated, tenant-scoped credentials (ABAC/token-vending) prevent cross-tenant access at runtime. | M02 storage model: shared object store with enforced tenant prefix (s3://platform-data/{tenantId}/...) as default, bucket-per-tenant offered as a premium isolation tier; all data-plane access uses short-lived credentials scoped to the caller's tenant prefix, so cross-tenant reads are impossible even with a leaked URL. | [Partitioning and Isolating Multi-Tenant SaaS Data with Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/apn/partitioning-and-isolating-multi-tenant-saas-data-with-amazon-s3/); [Design patterns for multi-tenant access control on S3](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/design-patterns-for-multi-tenant-access-control-on-amazon-s3/) |
| 10 | Google Zanzibar (and its open-source implementation SpiceDB) models authorization as a schema of object types, relations, and computed permissions over relationship tuples (e.g., document:readme#editor@user:emilia); a chain of relationships defines access, which subsumes both RBAC and ABAC and natively expresses hierarchy inheritance. | M03 models the platform → tenant → project → asset hierarchy as parent relations so permissions granted at a higher scope propagate downward by computation (not by copying grants); even if implemented in SQL rather than SpiceDB, the FDD's permission-resolution algorithm follows this relation-walk semantics. | [Google Zanzibar — AuthZed docs](https://authzed.com/docs/spicedb/concepts/zanzibar) |
| 11 | Two proven composition patterns: Kubernetes aggregated ClusterRoles build composite roles whose rules are auto-merged from atomic, label-selected component roles (the control plane overwrites manually set rules); Casbin's RBAC-with-domains makes every role assignment a (user, role, domain) triple so the same user holds different roles per tenant, with the matcher requiring domain equality. | M03 defines an atomic permission-point catalog (module:resource:action codes); roles are named unions of permission points (custom roles = recombination, mirroring aggregation); role bindings always carry a scope (platform / tenant / project), i.e., user–role–scope triples, never global roles. | [Kubernetes RBAC — Aggregated ClusterRoles](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/rbac/); [Casbin RBAC with Domains](https://casbin.apache.org/docs/rbac-with-domains/) |
| 12 | AWS IAM policy evaluation makes an explicit Deny absolute: if any applicable policy contains a matching Deny statement, evaluation returns Deny regardless of any Allows ("an explicit deny overrides an explicit allow"); there is no allow-that-overrides-deny anywhere in the model. | M03 permission resolution: union all role-derived allows, then apply explicit deny entries (e.g., restricted dataset/robot-fleet ACLs, suspended users, frozen tenants) as a final overriding pass — deny-overrides combining, evaluated deny-first for short-circuiting. | [AWS IAM Policy Evaluation Logic](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html) |

Key architectural references: OTP handling follows NIST SP 800-63B §5.1.3.2/§5.2.2 (≥20-bit single-use codes, ≤10-min validity, mandatory throttling) with hash-at-rest storage per AWS's OTP reference architecture, and session lifetimes follow SP 800-63B-4 AAL guidance (≤24 h absolute / ≤1 h inactivity for sensitive scopes). The tenant resource model mirrors Kubernetes' official multi-tenancy stack — namespace-per-tenant compiled into ResourceQuota + LimitRange for quota enforcement, taint/label node-pool binding for exclusive physical GPU assignment, and RayCluster-per-tenant (KubeRay) for logical compute instances, since Ray itself offers no intra-cluster tenant isolation. The permission model combines three proven systems: an atomic permission catalog composed into roles (Kubernetes aggregated ClusterRoles), tenant-scoped user–role–scope bindings (Casbin RBAC-with-domains), hierarchical scope inheritance via relationship semantics (Google Zanzibar/SpiceDB), and deny-overrides evaluation for restricted assets (AWS IAM policy evaluation logic).


#### 0.7.2 Robot Data Ingestion, Alignment & Canonical Episodes (M04–M06)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MCAP is the default rosbag2 storage format since ROS 2 Iron (2023), replacing SQLite3 (.db3): it combines SQLite3 "resilient"-mode data safety with write-optimized throughput, supports zstd/lz4 chunk compression, and is served via the pluggable `rosbag2_storage_mcap` plugin (legacy .db3 remains supported). | M04/M05 mandate MCAP as the canonical raw capture container; ingestion accepts legacy `.db3` and ROS 1 bags but normalizes everything to indexed MCAP on arrival. | [foxglove.dev/blog/mcap-as-the-ros2-default-bag-format](https://foxglove.dev/blog/mcap-as-the-ros2-default-bag-format), [github.com/ros-tooling/rosbag2_storage_mcap](https://github.com/ros-tooling/rosbag2_storage_mcap) |
| 2 | The MCAP spec is an append-only, self-contained container: Chunk Index + per-chunk Message Index records enable seeking and topic-subset reads without full scans; Attachment records carry auxiliary artifacts (e.g., calibration data, core dumps) and Metadata records carry key-value user data; heterogeneous serializations (ROS 1/ROS 2/Protobuf/JSON/FlatBuffers) can coexist in one file. | M05 rejects/repairs unindexed MCAP at ingest (summary section required); per-recording calibration snapshots and operator notes are embedded as MCAP attachments/metadata so raw files stay self-describing. | [mcap.dev/spec](https://mcap.dev/spec), [mcap.dev/guides/getting-started/ros-2](https://mcap.dev/guides/getting-started/ros-2) |
| 3 | Large-file upload practice: S3 multipart upload is recommended for objects ≥100 MB (5 MB min part, 10,000-part max, 16–64 MB parts typical), with per-part checksums (CRC32/CRC32C/SHA-1/SHA-256) plus server-side full-object checksum validation, part-level retry, and abort/resume of incomplete uploads; the tus protocol standardizes HTTP resumable upload (HEAD returns `Upload-Offset`, PATCH resumes; IETF draft) for unreliable links. | M05 upload gateway issues presigned multipart sessions with a per-file manifest (part list, per-part checksum, full-object SHA-256); edge agents on robots with flaky connectivity use tus-style offset-resume so interrupted uplinks never re-send received bytes. | [docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/mpuoverview.html](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/mpuoverview.html), [tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload](https://tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload) |
| 4 | RLDS (Google Research) defines the de facto episodic schema for sequential decision-making data: a dataset is a collection of episodes; each episode is a sequence of steps holding `observation`, `action`, `reward`, `discount`, plus boundary flags `is_first`/`is_last`/`is_terminal`; the format is lossless and transformation libraries derive either full-episode or randomized-step views. | M06's canonical episode schema mirrors RLDS step semantics (observation/action/boundary flags as required fields, reward/discount optional for teleop data) so exports to RLDS/TFDS are lossless. | [github.com/google-research/rlds](https://github.com/google-research/rlds), [research.google/blog/rlds-an-ecosystem-...](https://research.google/blog/rlds-an-ecosystem-to-generate-share-and-use-datasets-in-reinforcement-learning/) |
| 5 | Open X-Embodiment pooled 60 datasets from 34 labs (1M+ trajectories, 22 embodiments, 527 skills) by converting each into per-dataset RLDS episodes in serialized tfrecord files; the format deliberately accommodates differing action spaces and input modalities (varying RGB/depth/point-cloud cameras) per embodiment rather than forcing one unified schema at storage time. | M06 stores per-embodiment feature schemas (action space, camera set) in a registry and performs cross-embodiment normalization only at training-export time, never destructively at rest — the OXE lesson. | [robotics-transformer-x.github.io](https://robotics-transformer-x.github.io/), [github.com/google-deepmind/open_x_embodiment](https://github.com/google-deepmind/open_x_embodiment) |
| 6 | LeRobotDataset v3.0 (HuggingFace, shipped with lerobot v0.4.0, late 2025) moved from one-file-per-episode (v2.1) to file-based chunking: many episodes concatenated per Parquet file and per MP4 file, unified Parquet metadata (episodes, tasks, stats — no scattered JSONs), and streaming access — explicitly to reduce filesystem pressure and reach OXE scale. | M06 canonical store adopts the v3 layout: chunked Parquet for frame-aligned tabular data + concatenated MP4 for camera streams, episode index ranges in Parquet metadata; episodes are logical slices, not files. | [huggingface.co/blog/lerobot-datasets-v3](https://huggingface.co/blog/lerobot-datasets-v3), [huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/lerobot-dataset-v3](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/lerobot-dataset-v3) |
| 7 | HuggingFace's video-encoding study for robot datasets shows encoding camera streams as video (H.264/H.265/AV1, yuv420p) instead of per-frame images shrinks datasets to ~14% of original size on average with no measurable policy-performance loss; frame retrieval matches query timestamps to frames (L1-nearest) via torchcodec/pyav decoders. | M06 transcodes raw camera topics to AV1 (H.264 fallback) with keyframe intervals tuned for random access; per-frame decode timestamps live in the Parquet tables so loaders do timestamp→frame lookup, never frame-index guessing. | [huggingface.co/blog/video-encoding](https://huggingface.co/blog/video-encoding), [github.com/meta-pytorch/torchcodec](https://github.com/meta-pytorch/torchcodec) |
| 8 | ROS 2 `message_filters.ApproximateTimeSynchronizer` is the reference for multi-sensor alignment: it matches messages across topics by header timestamp within a configurable `slop` tolerance (seconds) and queue size (e.g., 100 ms slop = synchronized), with options for headerless messages and arrival-time sync. | M06's offline aligner reimplements approximate-time matching with per-stream configurable tolerance (default: half the slowest stream's period); frames whose constituent messages exceed tolerance are flagged, and episodes above a flagged-frame ratio fail alignment QA. | [docs.ros.org message_filters Approximate-Synchronizer tutorial](https://docs.ros.org/en/jazzy/p/message_filters/doc/Tutorials/Approximate-Synchronizer-Python.html) |
| 9 | Hardware timestamping via PTP/IEEE-1588 yields sub-microsecond clock sync across Ethernet sensors (vs millisecond-level NTP/software stamps); GigE Vision cameras support PTP natively, and real deployments mix PTP within a robot with NTP across robots — so per-message clock provenance matters. | M04 capture profiles require PTP-disciplined clocks on-robot where hardware allows; M05 records the clock domain and timestamp source (hardware sensor stamp vs driver receive time) per channel, and M06 prefers hardware stamps for alignment. | [teledynevisionsolutions.com IEEE-1588 PTP tech paper](https://www.teledynevisionsolutions.com/learn/learning-center/machine-vision/precision-system-synchronization-with-the-ieee-1588-precision-time-protocol-ptp/) |
| 10 | ROS convention separates calibration concerns: intrinsics (focal length, principal point, distortion) travel as `CameraInfo` messages published alongside images; extrinsics are tf2 static transforms/URDF fixed joints broadcast by `robot_state_publisher`; REP 105-style frame trees (base_link/odom/map) define coordinate semantics. | M06 treats a "calibration set" (per-camera intrinsics + extrinsic tf tree + URDF revision) as a versioned, first-class entity; every episode references the calibration-set ID active at capture, enabling re-projection and audit. | [docs.nav2.org/setup_guides/transformation/setup_transforms.html](https://docs.nav2.org/setup_guides/transformation/setup_transforms.html), [industrial-training-master.readthedocs.io Cameras-and-Calibration](https://industrial-training-master.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_source/session9/Cameras-and-Calibration.html) |
| 11 | Corruption tooling exists but is uneven: the `mcap` CLI offers `doctor` (full structural validation) and `recover` (salvage messages from truncated/corrupt files, e.g., crash-interrupted recordings), whereas `ros2 bag reindex` rebuilds metadata.yaml by reading every message and has known per-distro reliability gaps (e.g., Humble). | M05 runs `mcap doctor`-equivalent structural validation on every uploaded file as the first quality gate; truncated files from on-robot crashes go through automated `mcap recover` with the salvage delta logged, instead of silent rejection. | [mcap.dev/guides/cli](https://mcap.dev/guides/cli), [docs.ros.org rosbag2_storage_mcap](https://docs.ros.org/en/humble/p/rosbag2_storage_mcap/) |
| 12 | The Write-Audit-Publish (WAP) pattern, natively supported by Apache Iceberg branching, stages new data on an audit branch invisible to consumers, runs declarative quality checks (e.g., AWS Glue Data Quality), then publishes via `fast_forward` to main — quarantining bad data instead of letting it pollute production tables; known cost is added latency. | M05/M06 adopt WAP end-to-end: ingested recordings and derived episodes land in a quarantine/audit state, pass completeness checks (expected topic set, frequency/gap detection vs campaign profile, checksum match) before atomic publish to the searchable catalog; failures route to a triage queue. | [aws.amazon.com WAP with Iceberg branching blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/build-write-audit-publish-pattern-with-apache-iceberg-branching-and-aws-glue-data-quality/), [dremio.com WAP branching blog](https://www.dremio.com/blog/streamlining-data-quality-in-apache-iceberg-with-write-audit-publish-branching/) |

Key architectural references: **Foxglove's MCAP-native data platform** (out-of-order ingestion, indexing, and topic-level retrieval over MCAP/ROS bags — [foxglove.dev/product/data-management](https://foxglove.dev/product/data-management)) is the closest commercial analogue for M05's ingestion/indexing tier; **HuggingFace LeRobotDataset v3.0** (chunked Parquet + concatenated AV1/MP4 + Parquet-unified metadata with streaming) is the reference layout for M06's canonical episode store; and **Apache Iceberg WAP branching with AWS Glue Data Quality** is the reference quarantine/publish mechanism for M05's quality gating. RLDS/Open X-Embodiment conventions govern the export-schema contract so platform episodes remain losslessly convertible to the formats the research community trains on.


#### 0.7.3 Annotation, QA & Dataset Governance (M07–M08)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Label Studio supports importing model outputs as `predictions` (pre-annotations) in a documented JSON format, plus an ML backend that serves a `/predict` endpoint for both batch pre-labeling and interactive smart-tool assistance; annotators correct pre-labels instead of labeling from scratch. | M07 pre-label service writes detector/VLM outputs in Label-Studio-compatible prediction JSON (with per-prediction `score` and model version); annotation UI consumes pre-annotations by default, and an ML-backend-style `/predict` API powers interactive assist. | https://labelstud.io/guide/ml, https://labelstud.io/guide/predictions |
| 2 | CVAT runs auto-annotation models (incl. SAM interactor and SAM2 tracker) as Nuclio serverless functions deployed independently of the UI, with CPU/GPU variants; SAM2 tracking keeps state in Redis, enabling click-to-mask and mask propagation across video frames. | Auto-annotation models deployed as independently scalable GPU inference functions (serverless/K8s) decoupled from the annotation frontend; SAM-family interactors are the baseline for mask + track-propagation assist on robot episode video. | https://docs.cvat.ai/docs/guides/serverless-tutorial/ |
| 3 | SageMaker Ground Truth sends each object to N workers and consolidates via built-in algorithms (majority voting; Expectation-Maximization / modified Dawid-Skene that weights annotators by estimated accuracy; Jaccard-similarity clustering for spans), with custom consolidation pluggable as a Lambda (`AnnotationConsolidationConfig`). | M07 supports configurable overlap (N≥2 for safety-critical labels) with a pluggable consolidation stage: majority vote default, Dawid-Skene-style annotator-accuracy weighting for class labels, IoU-cluster merging for boxes/masks; consolidation runs as a pipeline hook, not in the UI. | https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/sms-annotation-consolidation.html |
| 4 | NVIDIA TAO Data Services auto-label uses GroundingDINO (open-vocabulary detection from text prompts/class names) to generate bounding boxes and MAL (transformer-based Mask Auto Labeler) to generate instance masks from boxes only — the productized form of the dominant 2025–26 VLM-assisted labeling pattern (open-vocab detector + SAM/MAL densification + human verify). | Two-stage auto-label pipeline: ontology class names → open-vocabulary detector proposes boxes → mask auto-labeler densifies → results enter a human verification queue. Humans verify/correct rather than draw; full manual labeling reserved for low-confidence/novel classes. | https://docs.nvidia.com/tao/tao-toolkit/latest/text/data_services/auto-label.html |
| 5 | Standard inter-annotator agreement metrics: Cohen's kappa (2 raters), Fleiss' kappa (>2 raters, equal counts), Krippendorff's alpha (handles missing data, variable annotator counts, nominal/ordinal/interval/ratio data). Accepted thresholds: α ≥ 0.80 production-grade, 0.667–0.80 usable with caution, < 0.667 indicates guideline/training defects. | M07 computes Krippendorff's alpha per label type per batch (robust to variable overlap); dataset release gate at α ≥ 0.8, mandatory guideline review for 0.67–0.8, batch blocked and requeued below 0.67. Kappa reported per annotator pair for performance management. | https://encord.com/blog/interrater-reliability-krippendorffs-alpha/ |
| 6 | CVAT quality control uses a Ground Truth job per task (hidden expert-labeled frames) and a Honeypot mode that re-embeds validated frames across jobs to reach 5–10% validation coverage without extra expert effort; annotators cannot tell which frames are gold; per-task quality reports show score, conflicts, and per-job breakdown. | Gold tasks/honeypots adopted as continuous QA: 3–10% hidden gold frames injected into every annotation job; automatic comparison yields per-annotator accuracy scores and conflict reports that drive requeue, retraining, or annotator offboarding — distinct from one-time qualification tests at onboarding. | https://docs.cvat.ai/docs/qa-analytics/auto-qa/ |
| 7 | Label Studio Enterprise computes task agreement via consensus (most-common-answer) or pairwise matching; review queues can be ordered by ascending agreement so reviewers see contested tasks first; low-agreement tasks can auto-assign additional annotators; strict overlap enforcement is a project setting. | Adjudication workflow: review queue sorted by agreement (lowest first), automatic escalation of low-agreement tasks to an extra annotator, then to a senior adjudicator whose decision is terminal; reviewer role separated from annotator role with accept/reject/fix actions logged. | https://docs.humansignal.com/guide/stats.html |
| 8 | lakeFS provides Git semantics (branch/commit/merge/tag) over object stores (S3/GCS/MinIO); branches are zero-copy metadata operations at any scale, and every commit is an immutable, tamper-proof snapshot enabling exact reproduction of training inputs; DVC achieves versioning with Git-tracked metafiles plus a content-addressable cache where files are stored by hash (`dvc.lock` pins exact hashes per pipeline stage). | M08 dataset snapshots = immutable commits over the object-store curated zone (lakeFS-style zero-copy branching for curation experiments); content-addressable hashing (DVC-style) dedupes episode artifacts across snapshots; a training run references exactly one commit ID/tag. | https://docs.lakefs.io/, https://doc.dvc.org/user-guide/pipelines |
| 9 | Delta Lake time travel queries prior table versions via `VERSION AS OF` / `TIMESTAMP AS OF` from the append-only transaction log, but is bounded by retention: `delta.logRetentionDuration` defaults to 30 days and VACUUM removes unreferenced data files after 7 days by default — after which time travel fails, so it is not an archival mechanism. | Engine-level time travel treated as operational convenience only; training/release snapshots are pinned by explicit immutable tags + manifests exempted from VACUUM/retention policies, with documented retention windows per dataset tier — never rely on default time travel for compliance reproducibility. | https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta/history |
| 10 | OpenLineage is the LF AI & Data open standard for lineage: Run events (START/COMPLETE/FAIL/ABORT) link Jobs to input/output Datasets, all extensible via facets (schema, column stats, data-quality assertions, source-code version, custom facets); Marquez is the reference implementation that collects and visualizes the lineage graph. | Every M05/M06/M07 pipeline stage emits OpenLineage run events with custom facets (robot embodiment, sensor-config hash, annotation-guideline version); lineage sink makes any model checkpoint traceable back through dataset snapshot commits to raw episode ingest. | https://openlineage.io/docs/ |
| 11 | DataHub's metadata model is schema-first (Pegasus PDL): Entities (Dataset, CorpUser, ML Model…) are graph nodes identified by URNs, described by Aspects (smallest atomic write unit); lineage is modeled dataset-to-dataset or via dataJob input/output, including fine-grained field-level lineage. | M08 catalog adopts the entity/aspect/URN metamodel: episodes, dataset snapshots, annotation projects, and models as first-class entities with custom aspects (embodiment, sensor suite, license, QA scores); URN scheme `urn:li:dataset:(platform,name,env)` adapted to `robot-episode`/`snapshot` types. | https://docs.datahub.com/docs/metadata-modeling/metadata-model |
| 12 | Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2018) prescribe documenting motivation, composition, collection process, labeling, recommended uses, distribution, and maintenance; Hugging Face operationalizes this as dataset cards (README.md with machine-readable YAML: license, language, size, tags) and enforces usage restrictions via gated datasets — file access requires terms/license acceptance, manual or automatic approval, optional geo-restriction, with all access requests reviewable via UI/API. | Every published snapshot ships a mandatory machine-readable dataset card (datasheet fields + license/consent/usage-restriction YAML) generated partly from catalog metadata; restricted datasets are gated behind terms acceptance with an approval workflow, and every access grant/denial is written to an immutable audit trail. | https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/datasets-cards, https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/datasets-gated |

Key architectural references: The M07 design follows the **Label Studio ML-backend pre-annotation pattern** (predictions JSON + `/predict` interactive assist, with SageMaker-Ground-Truth-style pluggable multi-worker consolidation) combined with **CVAT's Ground-Truth-job/honeypot QA model** (hidden gold frames, per-annotator quality scores, agreement-ordered review queues gated on Krippendorff's α ≥ 0.8). The M08 design follows the **lakeFS immutable-commit/tag snapshot pattern over object storage**, with every pipeline stage emitting **OpenLineage** events into a **DataHub-style entity/aspect/URN catalog** that renders Hugging-Face-style gated dataset cards for license and usage-restriction enforcement.


#### 0.7.4 Hybrid Retrieval, Distribution Analytics & 4D Workbench (M18–M19)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Milvus always applies **pre-filtering** (scalar filter first, then ANN over surviving rows) and offers two modes: standard filtering and **iterative filtering** (ANN iterator + per-hit scalar check until topK is filled, better for highly selective filters). Scalar fields get dedicated indexes (Trie for VARCHAR, sorted index for numerics); Milvus 2.5 added BM25 full-text search alongside vectors. | M18 search API contract is pre-filter-then-ANN by default; scalar fields (robot ID, site, task, skill tag, date, QA status) are index-declared at collection creation; iterative-filter mode auto-selected when filter selectivity is high. | [milvus.io/docs/filtered-search.md](https://milvus.io/docs/filtered-search.md) |
| 2 | Qdrant's **filterable HNSW** conditions graph construction on payload indexes: it builds per-payload-value subgraphs merged into the main graph so filtered traversal stays connected; a per-segment query planner picks filtered-graph-traversal vs brute-force full scan based on filter cardinality (`full_scan_threshold`). Payload indexes should exist **before** ingest so extra edges can be generated. | Filterable-graph pattern adopted: filterable metadata fields must be registered in the collection schema before bulk ingest; query planner with cardinality-based full-scan fallback specified as functional requirement for the retrieval service. | [qdrant.tech/articles/vector-search-filtering/](https://qdrant.tech/articles/vector-search-filtering/) |
| 3 | Weaviate hybrid search runs BM25 keyword and dense vector search **in parallel** and merges via two fusion algorithms — rankedFusion (Reciprocal Rank Fusion, robust to incomparable score scales) and relativeScoreFusion — with an `alpha` weight (0 = pure keyword, 1 = pure vector). | Episode/annotation text search (task descriptions, operator notes, auto-captions) fused with embedding search via RRF; `alpha` exposed as a query parameter and per-use-case preset (default ~0.5–0.7). | [weaviate.io/blog/hybrid-search-fusion-algorithms](https://weaviate.io/blog/hybrid-search-fusion-algorithms) |
| 4 | pgvector 0.8.0 added **iterative index scans** (`hnsw.iterative_scan = strict_order \| relaxed_order`, bounded by `hnsw.max_scan_tuples`, default 20,000) to fix the "filtered top-K underfill" problem, and `halfvec` (fp16) scalar quantization halving storage and raising the indexable dimension limit to 4,000. | pgvector used for small/medium embedding columns colocated with relational metadata (avoids a second store below ~10M rows); iterative-scan guardrails adopted so filtered similarity queries never silently return fewer than K results. | [github.com/pgvector/pgvector](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector) |
| 5 | LanceDB's **multimodal lakehouse** builds on the open Lance columnar format (file + table format + catalog on object storage): ~100x faster random access than Parquet, native data versioning, and vector + BM25 full-text + SQL filtering on the **same table**, scaling to petabyte multimodal data incl. video and point clouds. | Lakehouse single-copy pattern: clip-level embeddings, scalar attributes and blob references kept in one versioned columnar dataset on object storage, so analytics SQL, similarity search and training dataloaders read the same source. | [lancedb.com/blog/multimodal-lakehouse](https://www.lancedb.com/blog/multimodal-lakehouse) |
| 6 | ANN index trade-off is well characterized: HNSW gives lowest latency but must hold graph+vectors in RAM (practical to ~100M vectors); **DiskANN** serves billion-point indexes from NVMe SSD on a 64 GB-RAM machine at ~5,000 QPS with >95% recall@1 and low-ms latency; IVF(+PQ) is most compact and can win when filters eliminate most candidates. | Tiered index policy per collection: HNSW for hot, frequently queried collections; DiskANN-class disk-resident index for cold full-corpus search; IVF-PQ option for heavily filtered archival queries — selected by collection size/SLA, not hardcoded. | [github.com/microsoft/DiskANN](https://github.com/microsoft/DiskANN) |
| 7 | FiftyOne Brain provides `compute_visualization()` (UMAP/t-SNE/PCA projection of image/clip embeddings into an interactive 2D scatter that is **lasso-linked to the sample grid**) and similarity indexes with `sort_by_similarity()` plus natural-language search, over pluggable vector backends (Qdrant, Milvus, LanceDB, Elasticsearch, etc.). | M18 embedding-atlas UX adopted: 2D projection panel with lasso selection that filters the episode/clip grid in real time; similarity-sort and text-to-clip search wired to the platform's own vector store via a backend adapter. | [docs.voxel51.com/user_guide/brain.html](https://docs.voxel51.com/user_guide/brain.html) |
| 8 | NVIDIA NeMo Curator's video pipeline: clip splitting (fixed-stride or scene-change), clip-level embeddings via InternVideo2 or Cosmos-Embed1, motion/aesthetic filtering, then **semantic dedup** = k-means clustering of embeddings + intra-cluster pairwise cosine similarity, keeping one representative per near-duplicate group; stages run distributed on Ray. | Embedding pipeline pattern adopted: clip→embed→cluster→threshold dedup as a scheduled mining job over new teleop/video ingests; dedup verdicts written back as queryable sample attributes rather than hard deletes. | [docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator/25.09/curate-video/tutorials/split-dedup.html](https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator/25.09/curate-video/tutorials/split-dedup.html) |
| 9 | **HDBSCAN** is the preferred density-based clusterer for embedding-space dataset analysis: no preset k, handles variable-density clusters, and explicitly labels **noise points** — which in AV/robotics curation practice are treated as candidate rare/edge-case scenarios; GPU-accelerated in RAPIDS cuML for large corpora, and demonstrated directly over Milvus-stored embeddings. | Coverage-gap discovery job: HDBSCAN over episode embeddings per task family; sparse clusters and noise points surface as "underrepresented scenario" reports feeding collection-planning (M18 → data-ops loop); cuML used for >1M-clip runs. | [milvus.io/docs/hdbscan_clustering_with_milvus.md](https://milvus.io/docs/hdbscan_clustering_with_milvus.md) |
| 10 | Rerun is a time-aware entity/component store + viewer for multimodal robot data: ingests multi-rate streams (images, depth, point clouds, transforms, joint states, time series, video) from MCAP/rrd/LeRobot and renders them on a synchronized scrubbable timeline; **Blueprint API** defines viewer layouts from code (Blueprint → Horizontal/Vertical/Grid/Tabs containers → Spatial2D/3D, TimeSeries, TextLog views) for reproducible, data-driven layouts. | M19 4D workbench core pattern: entity-path data model with one global timeline; review layouts ("teleop QA", "policy-rollout triage") shipped as versioned blueprint-as-code templates generated per task type. | [rerun.io/docs/overview/what-is-rerun](https://rerun.io/docs/overview/what-is-rerun), [rerun.io/docs/concepts/visualization/blueprints](https://rerun.io/docs/concepts/visualization/blueprints) |
| 11 | Foxglove (browser-based) offers 20+ panel types — 3D scene (point clouds, markers, URDF, camera overlays with bounding boxes), image/video, plots, maps, state transitions, tables — all synchronized to one playback timeline with pause/step/speed control; MCAP is its open container format for multimodal logs. | Panel/layout system synchronized to a single global clock adopted for M19; MCAP fixed as the log interchange format; 3D panel requirements include trajectory overlays and workspace/sensor heatmap layers with shareable named layouts per review workflow. | [foxglove.dev/product/visualization](https://foxglove.dev/product/visualization) |
| 12 | FiftyOne's Model Evaluation panel renders predictions vs ground truth on the same sample, computes precision/recall/F1 and confusion matrices, supports click-through filtering (click TP/FP/FN or a class bar to isolate those samples), and **side-by-side comparison of two evaluation runs** to show which model version performs better. | Model-comparison review UX for M19: two policy/perception runs over the same episode set rendered side-by-side with synced timelines; metric deltas clickable down to the exact failing clips, feeding failure tags back into M18 mining queries. | [docs.voxel51.com/user_guide/evaluation.html](https://docs.voxel51.com/user_guide/evaluation.html) |

Key architectural references: (1) **Rerun** — entity/timeline data model plus blueprint-as-code is the reference architecture for the M19 4D workbench's synchronized multimodal replay and templated review layouts, with **Foxglove/MCAP** defining the panel-library and log-container conventions; (2) **LanceDB's Lance multimodal lakehouse** is the reference for keeping embeddings, scalars and raw sensor blobs in one versioned, SQL+vector+FTS-queryable store rather than a separate vector silo; (3) **FiftyOne Brain + Model Evaluation** (with **Qdrant-style filterable-HNSW** retrieval underneath and NeMo-Curator-style embed/cluster/dedup jobs) is the reference for M18's embedding-atlas curation, coverage-gap mining and side-by-side model review loop.


#### 0.7.5 Simulation, Synthetic Data & ModelOps/Evaluation (M09–M15)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Isaac Lab defines tasks via manager-based environments: a single `ManagerBasedRLEnvCfg` config class composes independent managers for scene, observations (grouped terms), rewards (weighted terms), terminations, events/randomization, curriculum and commands; environments are registered under Gym-style IDs. | M09 models a Scenario as a declarative, versioned config document (scene + embodiment + task terms + randomization + success criteria), not code; scenario IDs are registry keys so any sim/eval job is reproducible from config alone. | https://isaac-sim.github.io/IsaacLab/main/source/overview/core-concepts/task_workflows.html |
| 2 | ASAM scenario-based testing practice (BMW, Aptiv): driving scenarios are harvested from recorded sensor logs, exported to standardized ASAM OpenSCENARIO XML, accumulated into a scenario library, and replayed in simulators (e.g., esmini) for regression testing of every algorithm release. | M09/M15 adopt the AV "scenario library" pattern: every triaged field/sim failure is converted into a parameterized scenario record in the scenario catalog and added to a versioned regression suite that gates subsequent releases. | https://www.asam.net/application-stories/detail/the-role-of-asam-standards-in-scenario-based-testing-insights-from-bmw-and-aptiv/ |
| 3 | Omniverse Replicator implements domain randomization as composable randomizer graphs sampling object pose/scale, textures/materials, background, light count/pose/intensity, and camera position, with on-the-fly randomization per frame to diversify generated datasets. | M10 treats domain-randomization dimensions (pose, texture, lighting, background, camera, physics params) as enumerated, logged fields of a generation job spec, so every synthetic batch records exactly which axes were randomized and their sampling ranges/seeds. | https://docs.omniverse.nvidia.com/extensions/latest/ext_replicator/randomizer_details.html |
| 4 | NVIDIA Cosmos world foundation models (2025–26): Cosmos Predict 2.5 unifies Text2World/Image2World/Video2World generation (trained on ~200M curated clips); Cosmos Transfer 2.5 converts simulation/spatial-control inputs into photoreal variants for sim-to-real augmentation; Cosmos Reason is a physical-AI VLM used for data curation, captioning and outcome critique. 1X and Skild AI use them to augment humanoid training data. | M10 supports two synthetic generation backends as job types: procedural sim (Isaac/Replicator) and WFM-based generation/augmentation (Predict/Transfer-style), plus a VLM critic stage (Reason-style) that auto-scores generated clips before catalog ingestion. | https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/scale-synthetic-data-and-physical-ai-reasoning-with-nvidia-cosmos-world-foundation-models/ |
| 5 | C2PA Content Credentials cryptographically bind a provenance manifest (creator, tool, AI involvement, edit history) to media; OpenAI/Google attach them to AI-generated outputs; a known weakness is that platforms strip embedded metadata, motivating "durable" credentials combining manifest + watermark + fingerprint. | M10 marks every synthetic artifact with C2PA-style provenance: `synthetic=true`, generator model+version, source scenario/seed, randomization spec, parent real-data references — stored as catalog metadata (authoritative) and embedded sidecar (portable), so synthetic data can never silently mix into real-data quotas. | https://c2pa.org/ |
| 6 | MLflow Tracking organizes work into runs holding params, step-wise metric histories, tags and arbitrary artifacts; the `mlflow.data` module plus `mlflow.log_input()` attaches named, digested dataset versions to a run, giving end-to-end lineage from data source to model. | M11 records every training run with params/metrics/artifacts and a mandatory dataset-binding field: a run cannot start without resolved, immutable dataset-version IDs (name + digest), making "which data trained this model" a queryable fact, not tribal knowledge. | https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/ml/dataset/ |
| 7 | W&B Artifacts version datasets/models as content-addressed artifacts with automatic deduplication; runs and artifacts form a bipartite lineage graph (which run produced/consumed which artifact), so any deployed model traces back to the exact dataset version that trained it. | M11/M12 maintain a producer/consumer lineage graph (dataset version -> run -> checkpoint -> registered model version -> eval report) as first-class platform data, rendered as a navigable graph in the UI for audits and incident investigation. | https://docs.wandb.ai/models/artifacts |
| 8 | Ray on Kubernetes via KubeRay CRDs: a `RayJob` provisions an ephemeral RayCluster, submits the training job when ready, and tears down on completion; Ray Train scales PyTorch across workers with gang scheduling, fractional GPUs and placement groups. | M11 exposes training as a declarative job spec (image, code ref, dataset bindings, resources) submitted to a queue; the executor backend (RayJob or plain Kubernetes Job) is an infrastructure detail hidden behind the functional "submit/monitor/retry/cancel" contract. | https://docs.ray.io/en/master/cluster/kubernetes/examples/gpu-training-example.html |
| 9 | MLflow Model Registry deprecated fixed stages (None/Staging/Production/Archived) from v2.9 in favor of model version aliases — mutable named pointers like `@champion`/`@challenger` resolvable via `models:/<name>@<alias>` URIs, with multiple aliases per version — plus version tags (e.g., `validation_status: pending/passed`) for governance checkpoints. | M12 uses alias-based promotion instead of rigid stages: releases move the `production`/`champion` alias atomically (instant rollback = repoint alias), while validation/approval state is carried in structured tags written only by the M14 release workflow. | https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/ml/model-registry/workflow/ |
| 10 | Model cards (originating from Google's "Model Cards for Model Reporting", Mitchell et al.) are standardized on Hugging Face Hub as Markdown + YAML metadata declaring license, training datasets, metrics and structured eval results, intended use and limitations. | M12 auto-generates a model card per registered version from platform facts (bound dataset versions, training config, M13 eval results, intended embodiments/ODD, known failure modes); the card is a release artifact required by the M14 approval gate. | https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/model-cards |
| 11 | ExecuTorch compiles PyTorch models ahead-of-time via `torch.export()` into portable `.pte` programs running on a ~50 KB C++ runtime with 12+ hardware backends (Qualcomm, Arm, Apple, Vulkan...); reached 1.0 GA in late 2025. ONNX serves as the cross-framework interchange format, while TensorRT engine plans are GPU-architecture- and version-specific. | M14 treats packaging as metadata on a model version: each deployable package records format (.pte/ONNX/TensorRT engine), target hardware/backend, quantization scheme, and toolchain versions — since TensorRT engines are not portable across GPU generations, target-binding is mandatory, not optional. | https://docs.pytorch.org/executorch/stable/index.html |
| 12 | Isaac Lab-Arena (NVIDIA + Lightwheel, pre-alpha Feb 2026) is an open-source policy-evaluation extension to Isaac Lab: environments are composed on-the-fly from reusable building blocks (embodiments, objects, scenes, tasks), evaluation is GPU-parallel at scale, and it bridges community benchmarks such as LIBERO (4 suites x 10 tasks x 50 teleop demos; SOTA VLAs now exceed 95–98%, i.e., near-saturated) and RoboCasa. | M13 runs sim evaluation as massively parallel batch jobs over composable scenario x embodiment matrices, and versions its benchmark suites — because public suites saturate, the platform maintains internal, perturbation-augmented suite versions and reports scores always pinned to a suite version. | https://github.com/isaac-sim/IsaacLab-Arena |
| 13 | SIMPLER (Evaluating Real-World Robot Manipulation Policies in Simulation) showed sim evaluations with "visual matching" correlate strongly with real-world policy rankings (far better than offline validation MSE); follow-up work treats real2sim as prediction-powered inference: a small paired set of real+sim evals quantifies and corrects simulator bias. | M13 defines a three-tier evidence model — offline metrics < sim rollouts < real trials — and continuously maintains a paired real-vs-sim correlation dataset per task family; sim gates are only trusted (and tightened) where measured correlation is high. | https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.05941 |
| 14 | Robot policy failure-loop research: RoboFail systematically perturbs environments to discover and quantify policy failure modes; RoboGate demonstrates a closed loop of evaluate -> fine-tune on failures -> re-evaluate for VLA deployment; Sirius-Fleet continuously fine-tunes anomaly predictors from fleet interventions to cut human takeovers. | M15 implements the failure flywheel: fleet/eval failures are ingested as events, auto-clustered by embedding similarity into failure modes, triaged to root cause (data/model/scenario gap), then dispatched as either new collection tasks (M04/M05), new scenarios (M09) or regression-set additions (M13). | https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.02818 |

Key architectural references: The module group is patterned on four concrete stacks: (1) **NVIDIA Isaac Lab / Isaac Lab-Arena** for declarative scenario configs and composable, GPU-parallel policy evaluation, with **Omniverse Replicator + Cosmos WFMs (Predict/Transfer/Reason)** as the dual synthetic-data engines; (2) **MLflow** (Tracking with `mlflow.data` dataset binding + Model Registry with alias-based promotion replacing deprecated stages) as the canonical run/registry data model, with W&B-style content-addressed artifact lineage graphs; (3) **SIMPLER-style real2sim correlation calibration** to decide how much weight sim evaluation may carry versus real trials in release gates; and (4) the **ASAM OpenSCENARIO scenario-library regression pattern** from autonomous driving, which drives the M15 failure-to-scenario-to-regression-set flywheel.


#### 0.7.6 Orchestration, Compute Operations & Service Reliability (M16–M17, M20, Backend Services)

| # | Finding | Design pattern adopted | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apache Airflow 3 (GA 2025) adds native DAG versioning (AIP-65 "Improve DAG history", AIP-66 "DAG Bundles & Parsing"): each DAG run is bound in the metadata DB to the exact DAG structure/code version it started with and runs to completion on that version even if a newer version is deployed mid-run; UI shows per-run version, code, and logs. | M16 pipeline templates are immutably versioned; every work-request run pins a `template_version_id` at submission, and in-flight runs never silently pick up "latest" — version shown in run detail UI for auditability. | [airflow.apache.org](https://airflow.apache.org/blog/airflow-three-point-oh-is-here/) |
| 2 | Airflow 3 moved backfills into the scheduler (no longer separate CLI jobs): backfill runs follow the same scheduling, versioning, and observability model as regular DAG runs and can be started and monitored from the UI or REST API over a date range. | Rerun/backfill semantics for M16: historical reprocessing is a first-class API operation (`POST /work-requests/{id}/backfill` with date range), executed under the pinned template version with the same quota, retry, and observability path as normal runs. | [airflow.apache.org](https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow/stable/core-concepts/backfill.html) |
| 3 | Argo Workflows supports per-template `retryStrategy` (`limit`, `retryPolicy: Always/OnFailure/OnError`, backoff, and expression conditions over `lastRetry.exitCode/status/duration` to retry only transient errors) and exit handlers (`spec.onExit`) — a template guaranteed to run at workflow end regardless of success, failure, or error; step outputs flow as artifacts via S3-compatible storage. | Kubernetes-native execution layer for data pipelines: per-step retry policy distinguishes transient infra errors from deterministic failures; a mandatory exit handler posts terminal status + metrics back to the work-request service; artifacts land in tenant-scoped object-storage prefixes. | [argo-workflows.readthedocs.io](https://argo-workflows.readthedocs.io/en/latest/retries/), [walk-through](https://argo-workflows.readthedocs.io/en/latest/walk-through/retrying-failed-or-errored-steps/) |
| 4 | Temporal durable execution: a Workflow Execution runs deterministic workflow code "effectively once and to completion — whether it executes for seconds or years," reconstructed by replaying the Event History; all side effects live in Activities, which are auto-retried and whose recorded results are reused (not re-executed) on replay; Signals deliver asynchronous external events into a running workflow. | Long-running multi-stage work requests (ingest → QC → annotation → training → eval) modeled as durable workflows; every external call is an idempotent Activity with a retry policy; operator approve/cancel/priority-change delivered as Signals instead of polling. | [docs.temporal.io](https://docs.temporal.io/workflow-execution), [activity-execution](https://docs.temporal.io/activity-execution) |
| 5 | Kueue (Kubernetes job queueing): ClusterQueue governs a pooled resource quota; tenant-facing namespaced LocalQueues target a ClusterQueue; jobs stay suspended until their Workload is admitted against quota. Fair Sharing reclaims unused/borrowed capacity via preemption (since v0.13 candidate ordering uses LocalQueue usage); Admission Fair Sharing orders pending workloads by historical LocalQueue usage with configurable decay (`usageHalfLifeDecayTime`, `usageSamplingInterval`, `resourceWeights`) plus an entry penalty that blocks burst-submission gaming. | M17 GPU quota model: one LocalQueue per tenant namespace feeding shared per-GPU-flavor ClusterQueues; tenants may borrow idle capacity above nominal quota, reclaimed by preemption when the owning tenant returns; usage-history-weighted admission prevents heavy tenants from starving light ones. | [kueue.sigs.k8s.io](https://kueue.sigs.k8s.io/docs/concepts/cluster_queue/), [fair sharing](https://kueue.sigs.k8s.io/docs/concepts/fair_sharing/), [admission fair sharing](https://kueue.sigs.k8s.io/docs/concepts/admission_fair_sharing/) |
| 6 | Volcano batch scheduler: gang scheduling ("all or nothing") schedules a PodGroup only when its `minMember`/`minResources` can all be satisfied, preventing partially started distributed jobs from deadlocking GPUs; `priorityClassName` orders PodGroups within a Queue; Queues (FIFO collections of PodGroups) provide multi-tenant resource division, priority control, and preemption/reclaim. | Multi-node distributed training work requests (e.g., 8×GPU policy-learning jobs) run as gang-scheduled PodGroups so a job either gets its full GPU set or holds nothing — no idle-GPU deadlock; per-tenant queue priority for urgent fleet-incident retraining. | [volcano.sh](https://volcano.sh/en/docs/podgroup/), [queue management](https://volcano.sh/en/docs/queue_resource_management/) |
| 7 | OpenCost (CNCF incubating) defines a vendor-neutral spec for Kubernetes cost allocation: node cost = CPU + GPU + RAM + network cost; each container inherits per CPU-hour, GPU-hour, RAM GB-hour, and storage GB-hour rates from cloud billing APIs (AWS/Azure/GCP) or on-prem custom pricing; total asset cost = directly attributable workload costs + idle costs, forcing an explicit idle-allocation decision. | M17 cost attribution: per-tenant/per-work-request GPU-hour cost computed via label-based allocation following the OpenCost spec; idle GPU capacity reported as a separate platform-overhead line item in tenant cost dashboards rather than hidden in rates. | [opencost.io](https://opencost.io/docs/specification/) |
| 8 | Stripe idempotency pattern: clients send a generated `Idempotency-Key` header (V4 UUID recommended) on every POST; the server persists the first response (status code + body, even errors) keyed by that value and replays it for any retry of the same key, so a retried request can never perform the side effect twice; official client libraries layer automatic retries with exponential backoff on top. | All mutating platform APIs (work-request submission, export job creation, quota changes, notification sends) require an `Idempotency-Key`; responses cached per tenant+key with TTL; duplicate submissions return the original result instead of creating duplicate jobs. | [docs.stripe.com](https://docs.stripe.com/api/idempotent_requests), [stripe.com/blog](https://stripe.com/blog/idempotency) |
